attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old man presents to the Respiratory clinic for review. He migrated from Vietnam two months ago, at which time cavitating tuberculosis was found on chest X-ray and confirmed with positive sputum culture. He has a history of smoking but quit five years ago. He takes rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. He does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He continues to have a productive cough. His chest X-ray shows worsening cavitating lesions compared to two months ago.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation?
options: Sputum culture with drug susceptibility testing
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with worsening chest X-ray findings despite two months of standard first-line anti-tuberculosis therapy. This raises concern for either drug-resistant tuberculosis or poor medication adherence. Sputum culture with drug susceptibility testing is essential to guide further management by detecting the presence of viable mycobacteria and determining which antibiotics will be effective.

Bronchoscopy is an invasive procedure that carries infection risk to healthcare workers. It should not be performed without first attempting to obtain sputum samples, which are safer and less invasive.

Computed tomography scan of the chest may provide more detailed imaging of the cavities but would not help determine the underlying cause of treatment failure or guide antimicrobial therapy.

Mantoux tuberculin skin test and Interferon-gamma release assay are screening tests for tuberculosis infection. They cannot distinguish between latent and active disease, nor can they be used to monitor treatment response. These tests may remain positive even after successful treatment.

Drug-resistant tuberculosis is a significant global health concern, particularly in regions with high tuberculosis prevalence. Multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB) is defined as resistance to at least isoniazid and rifampicin, while extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis (XDR-TB) has additional resistance to fluoroquinolones and at least one second-line injectable agent.
learningPoints: Sputum culture with drug susceptibility testing is essential when tuberculosis treatment appears to be failing
Worsening cavitation on chest X-ray during tuberculosis treatment suggests possible drug resistance or poor adherence
Tuberculin skin tests and interferon-gamma release assays have no role in monitoring tuberculosis treatment response
references: Selvakumar, N, Rahman, F, Rajasekaran, S, Narayanan, PR & Frieden, TR 2002, ‘Inefficiency of 0.3% Carbol Fuchsin in Ziehl-Neelsen Staining for Detecting Acid-Fast Bacilli’, Journal of Clinical Microbiology, vol. 40, no. 8, pp. 3041–3043.

Sterling, T 2020, Treatment of drug-susceptible pulmonary tuberculosis in HIV-uninfected adults, UpToDate, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://www-uptodate-com.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/contents/treatment-of-drug-susceptible-pulmonary-tuberculosis-in-hiv-uninfected-adults?search=tuberculosis&source=searchresult&selectedTitle=3~150&usagetype=default&display_rank=3#H5>.‌

Bernardo, J 2020, Diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis in adults, UpToDate, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://www-uptodate-com.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/contents/diagnosis-of-pulmonary-tuberculosis-in-adults?search=tuberculosis&source=searchresult&selectedTitle=2~150&usagetype=default&display_rank=2#H340212714>.

Steingart, K, Flores, L, Dendukuri, N, Schiller, I, Laal, S, Ramsay, A, Hopewell, P & Pai, M 2011, Commercial serological tests for the diagnosis of active pulmonary and extrapulmonary tuberculosis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis, PubMed, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://pubmed-ncbi-nlm-nih-gov.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/21857806/>.
